description: Symptom: When wireless clients roam between access points connected to the same switch, they lose connectivity and receive an APIPA address. Connectivity is only restored after the MAC-age timer expires. Scenario: When a client is authenticated with a VLAN on a secure port, the VLAN is added as an ingress-only VLAN on other secure ports, but the MSTP state for those ports remains in blocked state instead of forwarding state, preventing seamless client movement. Workaround: Reduce the MAC-age timer to minimize the impact. or configure AP ports in "device-mode" instead of "client-mode":{color:#000000}interface 1/1/X port-access client-limit 1 port-access client-mode device{color}

description: Symptom: The switch is responding to ARP requests using an outdated IP address after the VLAN IP was updated. Scenario: In some rare scenarios, when the VLAN interface is in shutdown state and IP address/Active gateway IP/MAC is configured. After changing the IP address, the switch continues to use the old IP address as the source for ARP requests. Workaround: Configure the interface IP and Active gateway IP/MAC when the interface is UP and running.

description: Symptom: A VSF stack member switch unexpectedly reboots due to the ops-switchd process crashing with a signal: 6 error. Scenario: In some scenarios with excessive ACL event logging, a crash may occur due to a failure in the system when processing a high volume of ACL log entries. Workaround: Minimize the use of the log keyword in ACL entries, especially on high-traffic rules. Consider using ACL counters instead of logging for monitoring traffic patterns.

description: Symptom: Missing ARP entries showing as "incomplete" in the ARP table, causing connectivity issues in remote VTEP. Scenario: This issue may occur in an EVPN VXLAN environment with VSX pairs when a VIP (Virtual IP) moves from one node to another node, MAC address aging occurs or after executing "clear mac-address" on the switch. Workaround: A temporary workaround is to add a static ARP entry for the affected IP addresses.

description: Symptom: Frequent error messages in the debug log with the text "Invalid vlan map idx 4095.". Scenario: The messages appear when IP packets from the switch's internal processors are sent to the internal service lag, which has a native VLAN of 4095. These messages have no functional impact to the switch operation. The fix changes the severity level of these log messages from ERROR to DEBUG, making them less prominent in the logs. Workaround: N/A

description: Symptom: In an L2VPN network, when a DHCP client sends an IP request with the broadcast flag unset (unicast), duplicate DHCP client packets are observed at the DHCP server-one from each VTEP. As a result, the client does not receive an IP address. Scenario: To prevent duplicate DHCP client packets from each VTEP in an L2VPN topology, it is recommended to configure "no dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients" on the switch. However, after saving the switch configuration and rebooting, the relay daemon does not correctly apply all configuration settings at boot time, even though the configuration persists in the database. This causes the duplicate packet issue to reappear. Workaround: To restore correct behavior, manually enable and then disable the L2VPN client configuration using "dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients" followed by "no dhcp-relay l2vpn-clients".

section: Manual Configuration Restore for Software Downgrade
task: Manual Configuration Restore for Software Downgrade
procedure: Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example, DL.10.xx.yyyy).
Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config.
Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.

messages.content: For 10000 AOS-CX 10.16.1006, how do you restore configuration after a software downgrade?

Use the show checkpoint command to see the saved checkpoints and ensure that you have a checkpoint that is an exact match of the target software version (see the Image Version column in the output of the command, for example, DL.10.xx.yyyy). 2. Copy the backup checkpoint into the startup-config. 3. Boot the switch to the target version (lower version), making sure to select no when prompted to save the current configuration.

procedure: Copy the new image into the non-current boot bank on the switch using your preferred method.

Depending on the version being updated, there may be device component updates needed. Preview any devices updates needed using the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command and entering n when asked to continue.

When ready to update the system, if a non-failsafe device update is needed, make sure the system will not have any power interruption during the process. Invoke the allow non-failsafe updates command to allow updates to proceed after a switch reboot. Proceed to step 4 within the configured time.
Use the boot system <BOOT-BANK> command to initiate the upgrade. On the switch console port, output similar to the documented example will be displayed as various components are being updated.
